message.content:
You are an independent, adversarial fix-confirmation reviewer (reviewer A) for the hc-assay hardening campaign at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (branch harden/pass-10, latest commit). Confirm the fix is BOTH appropriate (correct root cause, sound approach, right layer) AND complete (no residual gap; regression test DISCRIMINATES — fails if reverted). Be skeptical; find holes.

FINDING P10-PII-1:
Operator LAN topology (gateway/host IP) leaked in committed transcripts; no IP rule. Fix in scripts/capture_transcripts.py _SECRET_PATTERNS: an RFC1918 regex (10/8, 172.16/12, 192.168/16) with (?<![\d.])...(?![\d.]) lookarounds, redacting private IPs (incl. a \n-glued form) while keeping 127.x, 0.0.0.0, and public IPs. Corpus re-scrubbed. VERIFY: _scrub redacts [REDACTED]/[REDACTED]/[REDACTED]/[REDACTED] and the glued form, keeps 127.0.0.1/0.0.0.0/160.79.104.10; grep -rlF the committed transcripts/ for the operator's real LAN IPs (192.168.50.x) → 0; the lookaround doesn't match inside a longer number (e.g. 1.10.0.0.5). Test: test_scrub_redacts_private_ips_keeps_loopback_and_public. Note source test files keep arbitrary example IPs (different subnet) — out of scope. Judge whether the operator-real network leak is closed and over-redaction is acceptable.

Read the changed files and run what you can (cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python -m pytest -q <path>; "python -m pytest"; gh for CI). Judge ONLY P10-PII-1. CONFIRMED only if appropriate AND complete; CONCERN if a real residual gap; REJECTED if wrong/non-discriminating. List concrete concerns (empty if none).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll review finding P10-PII-1. Let me start by examining the changed files.

d890ed3 fix(hardening-p10): 7 confirmed incl. 4 HIGH (2 security, 1 privacy, 1 provenance)
17b4eb5 docs(hardening): pass-9 merged (#170); open pass 10
a49b3f6 Merge pull request #170 from [REDACTED]/harden/pass-9
edbb576 docs(hardening): record pass 9 — actual-CI-execution + fix-regression each caught a HIGH
22cbcad fix(hardening-p9): wire ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS into the capture hook so [REDACTED] actually scrubs (P9-PII-1 concern)
8c5d7e5 fix(hardening-p9): 5 confirmed incl. security regression of pass-8 unix-socket fix
6e3d6b4 Merge pull request #169 from [REDACTED]/harden/pass-8
71ed4d5 fix(hardening-p8): CI follow-ons surfaced by actually running CI (K-CI-3 + lockfile via-comments)
---BRANCH---
harden/pass-10

1	#!/usr/bin/env python3
2	"""Capture the active Claude session's JSONL transcript(s) into the repo for provenance.
3	
4	Run by the pre-commit hook (.githooks/pre-commit) so every commit pulls in an up-to-date
5	snapshot of the agent interactions/effort behind it — an auditable record alongside the work.
6	
7	What it captures: the *active* session (the most-recently-modified top-level transcript in the
8	Claude project dir) plus that session's ``<id>/`` subtree (subagents + workflow transcripts).
9	Other/unrelated sessions are left out. Credential-shaped secrets are redacted on the way in.
10	
11	Honest scope: transcripts still contain local filesystem paths and may contain the operator's
12	email and internal reasoning. If this repo is published, review/exclude ``transcripts/`` first
13	(see transcripts/README.md). The wheel never ships them (only ``src/assay_engine`` is packaged).
14	
15	Config: ASSAY_TRANSCRIPT_SRC overrides the source dir. Exits 0 on any error (never blocks a
16	commit).
17	"""
18	
19	from __future__ import annotations
20	
21	import datetime as _dt
22	import hashlib
23	import json
24	import os
25	import platform
26	import re
27	import socket
28	import sys
29	from pathlib import Path
30	
31	DEFAULT_SRC = Path.home() / ".claude" / "projects" / "-Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc"
32	DEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "sessions"
33	MANIFEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "MANIFEST.json"
34	
35	# Credential-shaped secrets to redact (defense). Pass 3 (#F-024) extends this to operator PII —
36	# email addresses and the home-directory username embedded in filesystem paths — so committing
37	# transcripts as provenance (the campaign mandate) does not also publish the operator's identity.
38	# Reasoning content and the path STRUCTURE remain (legitimate provenance); see the README caveat
39	# for the full public-release consideration.
40	_SECRET_PATTERNS = [
41	    re.compile(r"sk-ant-[A-Za-z0-9_-]{2,}"),  # any sk-ant- token incl. oat01 OAuth + placeholders
42	    re.compile(r"\bgh[posru]_[A-Za-z0-9]{20,}\b"),  # GitHub tokens
43	    re.compile(r"\bAKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}\b"),  # AWS access key id
44	    # bearer tokens incl. base64url/base64 JWTs — must cover + / = or the token is split at the
45	    # first such char and only the leading fragment is redacted (pass 3, #F-051).
46	    re.compile(r"(?i)bearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9._\-+/=]{20,}"),
47	    # explicit OAuth/token env values: "...OAUTH_TOKEN":"<value>" / ...KEY=<value>
48	    re.compile(r'((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)"\s*:\s*")[^"]{8,}(")'),
49	    re.compile(r"((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)=)[^\s\"']{8,}"),
50	    # operator PII (#F-024): email addresses, and the username segment of a home path
51	    # (/Users/[REDACTED] /home/[REDACTED] — the path STRUCTURE is kept, only the identity is masked.
52	    re.compile(r"\b[A-Za-z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}\b"),  # email → [REDACTED]
53	    re.compile(r"(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s\"']+"),  # /Users/[REDACTED] → /Users/[REDACTED]    # The Claude project-dir SLUG (e.g. -Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc): redact the username segment
55	    # regardless of length (#H-018) — the bare-token rule below skips short handles to avoid
56	    # over-redacting common words, but the slug form is unambiguous PII at any length.
57	    re.compile(r"(-(?:Users|home)-)[^-\s/\"']+"),  # -Users-[REDACTED]- → -Users-[REDACTED]-
58	    # Private (RFC1918) IPv4 addresses (#P10-PII-1): captured ifconfig/route/lsof output leaks the
59	    # operator's LAN subnet/gateway/host IP (e.g. [REDACTED], gateway [REDACTED]) — infra-
60	    # identifying topology no other rule covers. Redact 10/8, 172.16/12, 192.168/16. Loopback
61	    # (127.0.0.0/8) and 0.0.0.0 are NOT private/identifying and are left (they appear in guard
62	    # tests/messages); public IPs (e.g. remote API endpoints) are not the operator's and are left.
63	    # Digit/dot lookarounds (not \b): the IP often abuts a JSON-escaped newline ("...\\n192.168...")
64	    # where \b fails because the escape's 'n' is a word char; the lookarounds still bound it so it
65	    # never matches inside a longer number/IP.
66	    re.compile(
67	        r"(?<![\d.])(?:10\.\d{1,3}\.\d{1,3}\.\d{1,3}"
68	        r"|172\.(?:1[6-9]|2\d|3[01])\.\d{1,3}\.\d{1,3}"
69	        r"|192\.168\.\d{1,3}\.\d{1,3})(?![\d.])"
70	    ),  # RFC1918 → [REDACTED]
71	]
72	
73	
74	def _username_pattern() -> re.Pattern[str] | None:
75	    """A redaction for the bare operator username wherever it appears (#G-007).
76	
77	    The /Users|/home path rule only masks the username inside a path; the same handle leaks via
78	    git author strings, prose, and the Claude project-dir slug (``-Users-[REDACTED]-...``). Derive the
79	    handle from the home directory and redact it as a standalone token too. Skipped for trivially
80	    short/None handles (e.g. a CI 'root') to avoid over-redacting common words.
81	    """
82	    try:
83	        name = Path.home().name
84	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - never break capture over a missing home
85	        return None
86	    if not name or len(name) < 4:
87	        return None
88	    return re.compile(re.escape(name))
89	
90	
91	def _display_name_patterns() -> list[re.Pattern[str]]:
92	    """Redactions for the operator's full DISPLAY NAME (#J-002).
93	
94	    The username/email rules leave the real name (e.g. a git ``user.name`` like "[REDACTED]")
95	    in cleartext — distinct operator PII that appears in git author strings, ``pyproject``
96	    ``authors=``, and prose, often right beside an already-redacted email. Derive candidate names
97	    from ``git config user.name`` and the ``ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES`` env (comma-separated), redact each
98	    (and, for a multi-word name, its whitespace-flexible form). Length-guarded to avoid
99	    over-redacting common words.
100	    """
101	    names: list[str] = []
102	    extra = os.environ.get("ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES", "")
103	    names.extend(n.strip() for n in extra.split(",") if n.strip())
104	    # Derive the operator name from SEVERAL sources — any one may be empty in a given context
105	    # (#J-002 follow-up): on this node local `git config user.name` is unset yet commits still
106	    # carry an author via global config / env, so a single-source lookup scrubbed nothing. Cover
107	    # the env author vars, git config, AND the actual last-commit author.
108	    for env_name in ("GIT_AUTHOR_NAME", "GIT_COMMITTER_NAME"):
109	        v = os.environ.get(env_name, "").strip()
110	        if v:
111	            names.append(v)
112	    try:
113	        import subprocess
114	
115	        for cmd in (["git", "config", "user.name"], ["git", "log", "-1", "--format=%an"]):
116	            out = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5)
117	            if out.returncode == 0 and out.stdout.strip():
118	                names.append(out.stdout.strip())
119	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - git absent/misconfigured must never break capture
120	        pass
121	    pats: list[re.Pattern[str]] = []
122	    seen: set[str] = set()
123	    for n in names:
124	        if len(n) < 4 or n in seen:
125	            continue  # too short → risks over-redacting common words; or already added
126	        seen.add(n)
127	        # Flexible inter-token whitespace (e.g. "[REDACTED]"). NO leading \b: the name often
128	        # appears glued to a JSON-escaped newline ("...---\\n[REDACTED]") where \b doesn't fire
129	        # because the escape's 'n' abuts the name (#J-002 follow-up). A trailing \b still prevents
130	        # matching inside a longer word (e.g. "Joneses"); the >=4-char guard bounds over-redaction.
131	        # CASE-INSENSITIVE (#J-002 confirm-concern round 2): the operator's name also appears
132	        # lowercased as actor/handle tokens — "[REDACTED]@hcgrc" (an initiated_by field), "[REDACTED]-[REDACTED]
133	        # (human)" in actor tables — which a case-sensitive pattern misses. re.IGNORECASE covers all
134	        # casings; "Joneses"/"joneses" stays protected by the trailing \b either way.
135	        toks = n.split()
136	        pats.append(re.compile(r"\s+".join(re.escape(t) for t in toks) + r"\b", re.IGNORECASE))
137	        # Redact each NAME TOKEN standalone too (#J-002 confirm-concern): the full-name pattern
138	        # leaves the operator's bare first/last name in cleartext — e.g. "[REDACTED] approves ..." and
139	        # "[REDACTED] builds and governs ..." appear in governance prose hundreds of times, uniquely
140	        # re-identifying the operator. Per-token redaction (len>=4, trailing \b, no leading \b for
141	        # the JSON-\n-glued case) closes it. Over-redaction is bounded: the tokens come only from
142	        # the verified operator name and the corpus contains no unrelated bearer of these tokens
143	        # (all 256 "[REDACTED]" contexts were sampled — every one is an operator governance reference).
144	        for t in toks:
145	            if len(t) >= 4 and t.lower() not in seen:
146	                seen.add(t.lower())
147	                pats.append(re.compile(re.escape(t) + r"\b", re.IGNORECASE))
148	    return pats
149	
150	
151	def _repo_handle_patterns() -> list[re.Pattern[str]]:
152	    """Redactions for the operator's repo owner / GitHub handle (#J-008).
153	
154	    The handle (the ``<owner>`` in ``github.com/<owner>/<repo>``, e.g. in PR/issue URLs and
155	    ``gh --repo <owner>/...`` commands) is operator-identifying PII that NO existing rule covers:
156	    it is not an email (the email rule needs an ``@``), and it differs from the home-dir username
157	    the bare-username rule derives. Left uncovered it leaked across hundreds of transcript files
158	    (#J-008). Derive candidate handles from the git remotes and the ``ASSAY_SCRUB_HANDLES`` env;
159	    redact each as a bare token (length-guarded). A handle is a single unambiguous string, so no
160	    word-boundary is needed — redact it inside URLs, ``gh`` args, and the ``<handle>@`` email stem.
161	    Matched case-insensitively, and the distinctive STEM (handle minus a generic ``the`` prefix) is
162	    also redacted, because the handle leaks via derived names — e.g. ``[REDACTED]-audience-profiles``
163	    (a skill name) embeds the handle minus ``the`` (#J-008 confirm-concern).
164	    """
165	    handles: list[str] = []
166	    extra = os.environ.get("ASSAY_SCRUB_HANDLES", "")
167	    handles.extend(h.strip() for h in extra.split(",") if h.strip())
168	    try:
169	        import subprocess
170	
171	        out = subprocess.run(["git", "remote", "-v"], capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5)
172	        if out.returncode == 0:
173	            for m in re.finditer(r"github\.com[:/]([^/\s]+)/", out.stdout):
174	                handles.append(m.group(1))
175	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - git absent/misconfigured must never break capture
176	        pass
177	    # Expand each handle with its distinctive stem (drop a leading generic "the") so handle-derived
178	    # names/slugs are covered too (#J-008 confirm-concern).
179	    expanded: list[str] = []
180	    for h in handles:
181	        expanded.append(h)
182	        if h.lower().startswith("the") and len(h) - 3 >= 4:
183	            expanded.append(h[3:])
184	    pats: list[re.Pattern[str]] = []
185	    seen: set[str] = set()
186	    for h in expanded:
187	        key = h.lower()
188	        if len(h) < 4 or key in seen:
189	            continue  # too short → risks over-redacting common words; or already added
190	        seen.add(key)
191	        if len(h) >= 8:
192	            # SEPARATOR-FLEXIBLE (#J-008 confirm-concern round 3): a GitHub handle is usually a
193	            # de-spaced brand ("[REDACTED]" = "[REDACTED]"); the SPACED source brand
194	            # appears in prose/links and lets a reader reconstruct the redacted handle/email by
195	            # concatenation. Allow punctuation/whitespace between each character so the spaced,
196	            # hyphenated, and dotted forms all redact — while the de-spaced form (empty separators)
197	            # still matches. The separator class is space/._- ONLY, so unrelated prose cannot
198	            # accidentally spell out the handle, and common single tokens (e.g. "CISO") are NOT
199	            # redacted because the WHOLE letter sequence (…[REDACTED]) must be present in order.
200	            pats.append(re.compile(r"[\s._-]*".join(re.escape(c) for c in h), re.IGNORECASE))
201	        else:
202	            pats.append(re.compile(re.escape(h), re.IGNORECASE))
203	    return pats
204	
205	
206	def _hostname_patterns() -> list[re.Pattern[str]]:
207	    """Redactions for the operator's machine/infrastructure hostname (#P9-MO/P9-PII-1).
208	
209	    The scrubber masked email/username/display-name/handle but had NO rule for the host the node
210	    runs on, so the production machine's hostname (e.g. ``[REDACTED]`` / ``[REDACTED]``) and
211	    internal ``*.local`` mDNS/namespace hosts leaked into committed transcripts — operator/infra
212	    identity. Derive the MACHINE hostname from ``socket.gethostname``/``platform.node`` (and, on
213	    macOS, ``scutil --get LocalHostName``/``ComputerName``) and redact both its bare and ``.local``
214	    forms (a machine name is distinctive). Additional internal hosts (e.g. an RDF-namespace host
215	    like ``[REDACTED]``) are supplied via the comma-separated ``ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS`` env and have
216	    ONLY their ``.local`` form redacted — the bare label (often a project name like ``hc-grc``)
217	    appears in legitimate paths/repo refs and must not be over-redacted.
218	    """
219	    machine: list[str] = []
220	    try:
221	        machine.append(socket.gethostname())
222	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001
223	        pass
224	    try:
225	        machine.append(platform.node())
226	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001
227	        pass
228	    try:
229	        import subprocess
230	
231	        for arg in ("LocalHostName", "ComputerName"):
232	            out = subprocess.run(["scutil", "--get", arg], capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=5)
233	            if out.returncode == 0 and out.stdout.strip():
234	                machine.append(out.stdout.strip())
235	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 - scutil absent (non-macOS) must never break capture
236	        pass
237	    extra = [h.strip() for h in os.environ.get("ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS", "").split(",") if h.strip()]
238	
239	    pats: list[re.Pattern[str]] = []
240	    seen: set[str] = set()
241	
242	    def _base(name: str) -> str:
243	        n = name.strip()
244	        return n[: -len(".local")] if n.lower().endswith(".local") else n
245	
246	    for name in machine:  # machine hostname → redact BARE + .local (distinctive identity)
247	        b = _base(name)
248	        if len(b) < 4 or b.lower() in seen:
249	            continue
250	        seen.add(b.lower())
251	        # Leading \b (#P10-PII-2): without it the bare hostname matches as a SUBSTRING, so a short/
252	        # common derived hostname (e.g. "host" on a CI runner) would corrupt legit content — "host"
253	        # inside "localhost" → "local[REDACTED]". The boundary requires a token start.
254	        pats.append(re.compile(r"\b" + re.escape(b) + r"(?:\.local)?\b", re.IGNORECASE))
255	    for name in extra:  # supplied namespace hosts → redact ONLY the .local FQDN form
256	        b = _base(name)
257	        if len(b) < 4 or ("local:" + b.lower()) in seen:
258	            continue
259	        seen.add("local:" + b.lower())
260	        pats.append(re.compile(re.escape(b) + r"\.local\b", re.IGNORECASE))
261	    return pats
262	
263	
264	_USERNAME_RE = _username_pattern()
265	_DISPLAY_NAME_RES = _display_name_patterns()
266	_REPO_HANDLE_RES = _repo_handle_patterns()
267	_HOSTNAME_RES = _hostname_patterns()
268	
269	
270	def _scrub(text: str) -> str:
271	    for pat in _SECRET_PATTERNS:
272	        if pat.groups == 2:
273	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]\2", text)
274	        elif pat.groups == 1:
275	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]", text)
276	        else:
277	            text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
278	    # Redact the bare operator username everywhere (#G-007), after the path rule so masked paths
279	    # don't re-expose it. Done last so it also catches it inside project-dir slugs / git authors.
280	    if _USERNAME_RE is not None:
281	        text = _USERNAME_RE.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
282	    # Redact the operator's full display name + bare name tokens too (#J-002) — distinct PII the
283	    # username/email rules leave in cleartext (git author strings, pyproject authors=, prose).
284	    for pat in _DISPLAY_NAME_RES:
285	        text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
286	    # Redact the repo owner / GitHub handle (#J-008) — operator-identifying PII in PR/issue URLs
287	    # and `gh --repo <handle>/...` commands that no email/username rule covers.
288	    for pat in _REPO_HANDLE_RES:
289	        text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
290	    # Redact the machine/infrastructure hostname (#P9-PII-1) — the production node's hostname and
291	    # internal *.local hosts that no other rule covers.
292	    for pat in _HOSTNAME_RES:
293	        text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
294	    return text
295	
296	
297	def _active_session(src: Path) -> Path | None:
298	    """The most-recently-modified top-level transcript = the live session."""
299	    candidates = sorted(src.glob("*.jsonl"), key=lambda p: p.stat().st_mtime, reverse=True)
300	    return candidates[0] if candidates else None
301	
302	
303	def _copy_scrubbed(srcf: Path, dstf: Path) -> dict | None:
304	    try:
305	        dstf.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
306	        raw = srcf.read_bytes()
307	        # Scrub EVERY text file, not only .jsonl (#CV-O-1): the session subtree also holds .json
308	        # workflow/subagent transcripts that carry the same operator PII + credential-shaped
309	        # secrets — the old suffix==".jsonl" gate copied those RAW, bypassing redaction entirely.
310	        #
311	        # FAIL CLOSED on redaction (#J-001): classify binary by an actual binary SIGNAL (a NUL
312	        # byte), NOT by UTF-8 validity. A transcript routinely embeds arbitrary subprocess stdout
313	        # (latin-1 dumps, truncated multibyte) inside JSONL strings; gating on strict decode meant
314	        # a single stray byte routed the WHOLE file to the verbatim branch and re-leaked it (the
315	        # CV-O-1 fix had turned an always-redact path into a fail-open one). Decode with
316	        # errors="replace" so redaction ALWAYS runs on text; only a genuinely binary blob (NUL
317	        # present) is copied verbatim.
318	        if b"\x00" in raw:
319	            data = raw  # genuinely binary — nothing to scrub
320	        else:
321	            data = _scrub(raw.decode("utf-8", errors="replace")).encode("utf-8")
322	        if dstf.exists() and dstf.read_bytes() == data:
323	            pass  # unchanged — still report it in the manifest
324	        else:
325	            dstf.write_bytes(data)
326	        return {
327	            "path": str(dstf.relative_to(DEST.parent.parent)),
328	            "bytes": len(data),
329	            "sha256": hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest(),
330	        }
331	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 — provenance capture must never break a commit
332	        return None
333	
334	
335	def main() -> int:
336	    src = Path(os.environ.get("ASSAY_TRANSCRIPT_SRC", str(DEFAULT_SRC)))
337	    if not src.is_dir():
338	        return 0
339	    session = _active_session(src)
340	    if session is None:
341	        return 0
342	    sid = session.stem
343	    captured: list[dict] = []
344	
345	    rec = _copy_scrubbed(session, DEST / f"{sid}.jsonl")
346	    if rec:
347	        captured.append(rec)
348	
349	    # the session's subtree: subagents/ + workflows/ transcripts
350	    subtree = src / sid
351	    if subtree.is_dir():
352	        for f in sorted(subtree.rglob("*")):
353	            if f.is_file():
354	                rel = f.relative_to(subtree)
355	                rec = _copy_scrubbed(f, DEST / sid / rel)
356	                if rec:
357	                    captured.append(rec)
358	
359	    try:
360	        MANIFEST.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
361	        MANIFEST.write_text(
362	            json.dumps(
363	                {
364	                    "captured_at": _dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc).isoformat(),
365	                    "active_session": sid,
366	                    "n_files": len(captured),
367	                    "total_bytes": sum(c["bytes"] for c in captured),
368	                    "files": captured,
369	                },
370	                indent=2,
371	            )
372	            + "\n",
373	            encoding="utf-8",
374	        )
375	    except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001
376	        return 0
377	    print(f"captured {len(captured)} transcript file(s) for session {sid}")
378	    return 0
379	
380	
381	if __name__ == "__main__":
382	    sys.exit(main())
383

content.text: Now let me look at the test and check the diff for this finding.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && git show --stat HEAD | head -40 && echo "===DIFF for capture_transcripts.py===" && git show HEAD -- scripts/capture_transcripts.py | head -120
input.description: Show commit stat and diff for capture script

content.content:
commit d890ed3cd1afc6f960ba9bfa434c16842d122fae
Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>
Date:   Wed Jun 17 13:34:33 2026 -0400

    fix(hardening-p10): 7 confirmed incl. 4 HIGH (2 security, 1 privacy, 1 provenance)
    
    All 2-agent verified TRUE_POSITIVE. Two are fix-regressions of pass-9's own fixes.
    
    - SEC-10-1 (high): libpq PGHOST/PGHOSTADDR/PGSERVICE env vars override/supply the host even
      when the DSN looks loopback (PGHOSTADDR wins over host=localhost) → graph state off-box
      despite require_local_uri passing. Fix: _assert_local_libpq_env() validates PGHOST/PGHOSTADDR
      (loopback/socket, comma-split) and rejects PGSERVICE/PGSERVICEFILE (a service file can
      redirect off-box, unvalidatable inline), called from get_postgres_connection_string.
    - P10-PII-1 (high): the operator's real LAN topology (gateway [REDACTED], host
      [REDACTED], interface) leaked into committed transcripts from captured route/lsof
      output — no IP rule. Fix: RFC1918 redaction (10/8, 172.16/12, 192.168/16) with digit/dot
      lookarounds (catches the \n-glued form, never matches inside a longer number); loopback
      127.x, 0.0.0.0 and public IPs preserved. Re-scrubbed corpus (literal private IPs now 0).
    - P10-CI-1 (high, fix-reg of CI-1): require_green_ci.sh checked only the latest run's
      conclusion, not its headSha → a stale green run certified a newer untested HEAD. Fix:
      fetch headSha and require it == git rev-parse origin/<branch>; fail closed if no completed
      run for the current commit.
    - P10-MO-1 (high): a set/frozenset payload is hashable but does NOT round-trip
      (unfreeze→list→re-freeze→tuple) so verify_records SILENTLY failed on a rebuilt chain. Fix:
      ProvenanceEntry rejects set/frozenset payload leaves loudly at record time (use a sorted
      list/tuple, which round-trips and digests identically).
    - P10-MO-2 (med): from_records raised a raw AttributeError on a non-mapping payload field.
      Fix: ProvenanceEntry raises typed ProvenanceError when payload is not a Mapping.
    - P10-PII-2 (low, fix-reg of P9-PII-1): the machine-hostname rule lacked a leading \b →
      substring over-redaction (host 'host' redacted inside 'localhost'). Fix: leading \b.
    - REL-1 (low, fix-reg of pass-8 K-REL-1): SIGTERM handler swallowed the signal when
      getsignal() returns None (prior handler installed from C). Fix: treat None like SIG_DFL and
      re-deliver the default terminate.
    
    Regression tests for every finding. Gates: ruff + format + mypy --strict clean; 575 passed,
    5 skipped; coverage floor enforced.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <[REDACTED]>

 scripts/capture_transcripts.py                     |  18 +-
===DIFF for capture_transcripts.py===

diff --git a/scripts/capture_transcripts.py b/scripts/capture_transcripts.py
index a94e434..bdaf66c 100755
--- a/scripts/capture_transcripts.py
+++ b/scripts/capture_transcripts.py
@@ -55,6 +55,19 @@ _SECRET_PATTERNS = [
     # regardless of length (#H-018) — the bare-token rule below skips short handles to avoid
     # over-redacting common words, but the slug form is unambiguous PII at any length.
     re.compile(r"(-(?:Users|home)-)[^-\s/\"']+"),  # -Users-[REDACTED]- → -Users-[REDACTED]-
+    # Private (RFC1918) IPv4 addresses (#P10-PII-1): captured ifconfig/route/lsof output leaks the
+    # operator's LAN subnet/gateway/host IP (e.g. [REDACTED], gateway [REDACTED]) — infra-
+    # identifying topology no other rule covers. Redact 10/8, 172.16/12, 192.168/16. Loopback
+    # (127.0.0.0/8) and 0.0.0.0 are NOT private/identifying and are left (they appear in guard
+    # tests/messages); public IPs (e.g. remote API endpoints) are not the operator's and are left.
+    # Digit/dot lookarounds (not \b): the IP often abuts a JSON-escaped newline ("...\\n192.168...")
+    # where \b fails because the escape's 'n' is a word char; the lookarounds still bound it so it
+    # never matches inside a longer number/IP.
+    re.compile(
+        r"(?<![\d.])(?:10\.\d{1,3}\.\d{1,3}\.\d{1,3}"
+        r"|172\.(?:1[6-9]|2\d|3[01])\.\d{1,3}\.\d{1,3}"
+        r"|192\.168\.\d{1,3}\.\d{1,3})(?![\d.])"
+    ),  # RFC1918 → [REDACTED]
 ]
 
 
@@ -235,7 +248,10 @@ def _hostname_patterns() -> list[re.Pattern[str]]:
         if len(b) < 4 or b.lower() in seen:
             continue
         seen.add(b.lower())
-        pats.append(re.compile(re.escape(b) + r"(?:\.local)?\b", re.IGNORECASE))
+        # Leading \b (#P10-PII-2): without it the bare hostname matches as a SUBSTRING, so a short/
+        # common derived hostname (e.g. "host" on a CI runner) would corrupt legit content — "host"
+        # inside "localhost" → "local[REDACTED]". The boundary requires a token start.
+        pats.append(re.compile(r"\b" + re.escape(b) + r"(?:\.local)?\b", re.IGNORECASE))
     for name in extra:  # supplied namespace hosts → redact ONLY the .local FQDN form
         b = _base(name)
         if len(b) < 4 or ("local:" + b.lower()) in seen:

220	def test_scrub_redacts_private_ips_keeps_loopback_and_public():
221	    # #P10-PII-1: captured ifconfig/route/lsof output leaks the operator's LAN subnet/gateway/host

226	        "gw [REDACTED] host [REDACTED] ten [REDACTED] priv [REDACTED] "
227	        "loop 127.0.0.1 any 0.0.0.0 pub 160.79.104.10 glued:\\n[REDACTED]"
228	    )

230	        assert priv not in out, f"private IP {priv} leaked"
231	    assert "127.0.0.1" in out and "0.0.0.0" in out and "160.79.104.10" in out  # kept
232	
233

236	    # match as a substring of legitimate content (e.g. host "host" must NOT redact "localhost").
237	    monkeypatch.delenv("ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS", raising=False)
238	    cap = _load("scripts/capture_transcripts.py", "capture_transcripts_hostsub")
239	    monkeypatch.setattr(cap.socket, "gethostname", lambda: "host")
240	    monkeypatch.setattr(cap.platform, "node", lambda: "host")
241	    cap._HOSTNAME_RES = cap._hostname_patterns()
242	    out = cap._scrub("connect to localhost; the localhostname var; rehosting the service")
243	    # leading \b prevents the SUBSTRING matches that were the defect: 'host' must not fire inside
244	    # 'localhost', 'localhostname', or 'rehosting'.
245	    assert "localhost" in out and "localhostname" in out and "rehosting" in out, (

247	    )
248	
249

251	    # #P9-PII-1 confirm-concern: the namespace host ([REDACTED]) only redacts when ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS
252	    # names it; the machine hostname auto-derives but this does not. The pre-commit hook must set
253	    # ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS before running capture, else every commit re-introduces [REDACTED].
254	    hook = (_ROOT / ".githooks" / "pre-commit").read_text(encoding="utf-8")
255	    assert "ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS" in hook, "pre-commit hook does not wire the namespace host scrub"

content.text: Now let me run the tests and verify the corpus scrub and the lookaround behavior claims.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && grep -rlF "192.168.50." transcripts/ 2>/dev/null | head; echo "exit_grep=$?"; echo "---count of any 192.168.50.x in transcripts---"; grep -rohE "192\.168\.50\.[0-9]+" transcripts/ 2>/dev/null | sort | uniq -c
input.description: Grep committed transcripts for operator LAN IPs

content.text: The grep found a remaining `[REDACTED]` in 7 files. This is a major concern — the finding's VERIFY claim says "grep -rlF the committed transcripts/ for the operator's real LAN IPs (192.168.50.x) → 0". Let me investigate. The IP appears glued in some context where the regex doesn't fire.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && grep -rohE ".{12}192\.168\.50\.134.{12}" transcripts/ 2>/dev/null | sort | uniq -c | head -30
input.description: Show context around the surviving IP

content.content:
4 rns exactly [REDACTED]. These are 
   3 rns exactly [REDACTED].\n\n3. The

content.text:
The surviving instances are inside *prose discussing the finding itself* (e.g. "returns exactly [REDACTED]."). The IP is followed by a `.` then space, or `.\n`. Let me check whether the regex should have caught these — the trailing `.` then non-digit.

The regex is `(?![\d.])` lookahead after the last octet. In `[REDACTED].` the char after `134` is `.`, which the negative lookahead `(?![\d.])` rejects — so the regex does NOT match `[REDACTED]` when followed by a period (sentence-ending period). That's the gap.

Let me confirm this directly.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python -c "
import importlib.util, pathlib
spec = importlib.util.spec_from_file_location('cap','scripts/capture_transcripts.py')
cap = importlib.util.module_from_spec(spec); spec.loader.exec_module(cap)
tests = [
  'returns exactly [REDACTED]. These are',
  'host [REDACTED] ten',
  'exactly [REDACTED].\\n\\n3. The',
  'addr [REDACTED], gw',
]
for t in tests:
    print(repr(t), '->', repr(cap._scrub(t)))
"
input.description: Test scrub on sentence-final IP forms

content.content:
'returns exactly [REDACTED]. These are' -> 'returns exactly [REDACTED]. These are'
'host [REDACTED] ten' -> 'host [REDACTED] ten'
'exactly [REDACTED].

3. The' -> 'exactly [REDACTED].

3. The'
'addr [REDACTED], gw' -> 'addr [REDACTED], gw'

content.text:
Confirmed. The trailing-dot lookahead `(?![\d.])` causes the regex to FAIL on a sentence-final IP `[REDACTED].` — exactly the form the operator's real host IP appears in across 7 committed transcript files. The fix is incomplete: the operator's real LAN host IP (`[REDACTED]`) is STILL present in the committed corpus, in 7 files.

Let me verify these aren't binary-skipped and confirm the leak is the operator's real IP (it matches the finding's stated real host IP). Also let me double-check the gateway and the other claimed IPs are clean.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && echo "===gateway [REDACTED]==="; grep -rohE "192\.168\.50\.1[^0-9]" transcripts/ 2>/dev/null | sort | uniq -c; echo "===any 10.x/172.16-31 RFC1918 survivors==="; grep -rohE "(^|[^0-9.])(10\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+|172\.(1[6-9]|2[0-9]|3[01])\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+)" transcripts/ 2>/dev/null | sort | uniq -c | head; echo "===total files with sentence-final private IP==="; grep -rlE "192\.168\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\." transcripts/ 2>/dev/null | wc -l
input.description: Check gateway and other RFC1918 survivors

content.content:
===gateway [REDACTED]===
===any 10.x/172.16-31 RFC1918 survivors===
===total files with sentence-final private IP===
       5

content.text:
The gateway IP and 10.x/172.x ranges are clean (those forms were followed by space/comma and got redacted). But the operator's real **host IP `[REDACTED]`** survives in 7 occurrences across multiple files because it appears in sentence-final position (`...134.`), where the `(?![\d.])` lookahead refuses to match.

This is precisely the operator-real network leak the finding claims to close — and it is NOT closed. The VERIFY claim "grep -rlF ... for 192.168.50.x → 0" is false.

Let me confirm these files are the committed (tracked) versions, not stray untracked content.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && git ls-files transcripts/ | xargs grep -lF "[REDACTED]" 2>/dev/null | head; echo "exit=$?"; echo "---git status of transcripts---"; git status --short transcripts/ | head
input.description: Confirm leak is in git-tracked files

content.text:
Confirmed: the operator's real LAN host IP `[REDACTED]` is present in git-tracked, committed transcript files. The working tree is clean (these are committed). The leak the finding claims to have closed is still live in the corpus.

Now let me assess the regression test's discriminating power. The test asserts `[REDACTED]` is redacted — but only in a context where it's followed by a space (`host [REDACTED] `), which the regex DOES catch. The test never exercises the sentence-final form that actually leaks. So the test passes while the real leak persists — non-discriminating for the actual corpus failure mode.

Let me verify the test would fail if the regex were reverted (i.e., it discriminates for *some* change), to characterize precisely.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python -c "
# Does the test discriminate against reverting the IP rule? The test's inputs are all space/comma/\n-bounded.
import re
# Simulate WITHOUT the IP pattern: the other rules. None of them touch bare IPs.
# So removing the IP rule => test fails. Confirmed it discriminates for the rule's existence.
print('test discriminates for rule existence: YES (other rules ignore IPs)')
# But it does NOT cover the sentence-final form that leaks in the real corpus.
"

content.content: test discriminates for rule existence: YES (other rules ignore IPs)

content.text: I have a definitive finding. Let me also verify the lookaround correctly handles the "longer number" claim (1.10.0.0.5) to be fair on what does work, then conclude.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && source .venv/bin/activate && python -c "
import importlib.util
spec = importlib.util.spec_from_file_location('cap','scripts/capture_transcripts.py')
cap = importlib.util.module_from_spec(spec); spec.loader.exec_module(cap)
for t in ['1.10.0.0.5','glued:\\\\n[REDACTED]','127.0.0.1','0.0.0.0','public 160.79.104.10']:
    print(repr(t),'->',repr(cap._scrub(t)))
"

content.text:
The keep-cases (loopback, 0.0.0.0, public), the \n-glued form, and the longer-number guard all work as claimed. The approach (RFC1918 regex, right layer in `_SECRET_PATTERNS`) is sound and over-redaction is acceptable.

But the fix is **incomplete**: the trailing `(?![\d.])` lookahead — added to avoid matching inside a longer number — also rejects a legitimate IP that ends a sentence (`[REDACTED].`). The operator's real LAN host IP appears in exactly that form and **survives in 7 occurrences across 4+ git-tracked transcript files**. The VERIFY claim "grep for 192.168.50.x → 0" is false; the operator-real network leak is NOT closed. The regression test passes only because its sample uses space-bounded IPs and never exercises the sentence-final form that actually leaks — so it does not discriminate against the residual gap.

input.rationale:
The approach is appropriate: an RFC1918 regex in _SECRET_PATTERNS is the right layer and root cause for the LAN-topology leak, and the keep-cases all behave correctly — loopback 127.0.0.1, 0.0.0.0, and public 160.79.104.10 are preserved; the \n-glued form (glued:\n[REDACTED]) is redacted; the longer-number guard works (1.10.0.0.5 untouched). Over-redaction is acceptable. HOWEVER the fix is INCOMPLETE and the VERIFY claim is false. The trailing negative lookahead (?![\d.]), added to prevent matching inside a longer number, ALSO refuses to match an IP that is immediately followed by a period — e.g. a sentence-final occurrence "...returns exactly [REDACTED]." The operator's REAL host IP ([REDACTED], named in the finding) appears in exactly that form and STILL survives in the committed corpus: grep -rohE counts 7 live occurrences across 4+ git-tracked files (e.g. transcripts/.../wf_646eb4f4-2cf/journal.jsonl, agent-a66a01f4d0c2201ac.jsonl, agent-ae9c9a30e88ec21b1.jsonl, workflows/wf_646eb4f4-2cf.json). I reproduced it directly: cap._scrub("returns exactly [REDACTED]. These are") returns the IP UNCHANGED. The finding's stated verification ("grep -rlF transcripts/ for 192.168.50.x -> 0") does not hold — it returns matching files. The gateway [REDACTED] and the 10.x/172.x ranges happen to be clean only because their corpus instances were space/comma-bounded. The regression test test_scrub_redacts_private_ips_keeps_loopback_and_public passes but is NON-DISCRIMINATING for this failure mode: every private IP in its sample is bounded by a space, so it never exercises the sentence-final ".134." form that actually leaks. The operator-real network leak is NOT closed.</rationale>
<parameter name="concerns">["Residual leak: the operator's real LAN host IP [REDACTED] is STILL present in the committed corpus in 7 occurrences across at least 4 git-tracked files (transcripts/sessions/8d54714a.../subagents/workflows/wf_646eb4f4-2cf/{journal.jsonl,agent-a66a01f4d0c2201ac.jsonl,agent-ae9c9a30e88ec21b1.jsonl} and workflows/wf_646eb4f4-2cf.json). Verified via grep and reproduced via _scrub.", "Root cause of residual: the trailing (?![\\d.]) lookahead rejects a private IP followed by a sentence-ending period (e.g. '[REDACTED].' or '[REDACTED].\\n'); _scrub leaves it verbatim. The lookahead conflates 'next char is a digit/dot of a LONGER number' with 'next char is a period of ordinary prose'. A better guard would require the following dot to be itself followed by a digit (i.e. only abort when it is genuinely a longer dotted-numeric), e.g. replace (?![\\d.]) with (?!\\d)(?!\\.\\d).", "The finding's VERIFY assertion 'grep -rlF the committed transcripts/ for 192.168.50.x -> 0' is FALSE on the actual branch; the corpus was not fully re-scrubbed for the sentence-final form.", "Regression test does not discriminate against the real defect: all sample IPs are space-bounded, so the test would still pass even though the live corpus leaks. Add an assertion that a trailing-period form (e.g. 'addr [REDACTED].') is redacted to make the test catch this gap."]

content.content: Output does not match required schema: root: must have required property 'concerns'

toolUseResult: Error: Output does not match required schema: root: must have required property 'concerns'

concerns: Residual leak: the operator's real LAN host IP [REDACTED] is STILL present in the committed corpus in 7 occurrences across at least 4 git-tracked files (transcripts/sessions/8d54714a.../subagents/workflows/wf_646eb4f4-2cf/{journal.jsonl,agent-a66a01f4d0c2201ac.jsonl,agent-ae9c9a30e88ec21b1.jsonl} and workflows/wf_646eb4f4-2cf.json). Verified via grep and reproduced via _scrub.

Root cause of residual: the trailing (?![\d.]) lookahead rejects a private IP followed by a sentence-ending period (e.g. '[REDACTED].' or '[REDACTED].\n'); _scrub leaves it verbatim. The lookahead conflates 'next char is a digit/dot of a LONGER number' with 'next char is a period of ordinary prose'. A better guard: only abort when the following dot is itself followed by a digit, e.g. replace (?![\d.]) with (?!\d)(?!\.\d).
The finding's VERIFY assertion 'grep -rlF transcripts/ for 192.168.50.x -> 0' is FALSE on the actual branch; the corpus was not fully re-scrubbed for the sentence-final form.

Regression test does not discriminate against the real defect: all sample private IPs are space-bounded, so the test passes even though the live corpus leaks. Add an assertion that a trailing-period form (e.g. 'addr [REDACTED].') is redacted to make the test catch this gap.
input.rationale: Approach is appropriate (RFC1918 regex in _SECRET_PATTERNS is the right layer/root cause; loopback, 0.0.0.0, public 160.79.104.10 preserved; \\n-glued form redacted; longer-number guard via 1.10.0.0.5 works; over-redaction acceptable). But INCOMPLETE: the trailing (?![\\d.]) lookahead also refuses to match an IP followed by a sentence-ending period. The operator's REAL host IP [REDACTED] appears in exactly that form and STILL survives in the committed corpus — 7 occurrences across 4+ git-tracked files (reproduced: _scrub('returns exactly [REDACTED]. These are') leaves it unchanged). The finding's VERIFY claim that grep for 192.168.50.x returns 0 is false. The regression test passes only because its sample IPs are space-bounded and never exercises the leaking sentence-final form, so it does not discriminate against the residual gap. The operator-real network leak is not closed.

content.text:
I have completed the adversarial review of P10-PII-1.

Verdict: CONCERN — the approach is appropriate but the fix is incomplete and the regression test does not discriminate against the actual failure mode.

Key finding: The operator's real LAN host IP `[REDACTED]` is still present in the committed corpus (7 occurrences across at least 4 git-tracked files under `transcripts/sessions/8d54714a-.../workflows/wf_646eb4f4-2cf/`). The trailing `(?![\d.])` lookahead — intended to avoid matching inside a longer number — also rejects an IP followed by a sentence-ending period (`...192.168.50.134.`), which is exactly the form the IP appears in throughout the prose. The finding's VERIFY claim "grep for 192.168.50.x → 0" is false. The regression test passes only because all its sample IPs are space-bounded, so it never exercises the leaking sentence-final form.

The fix should narrow the lookahead (e.g. abort only when the following dot is itself followed by a digit), re-scrub the corpus, and add a trailing-period case to the test.
